_metadata.description: Comprehensive database of harmful additives, contaminants, and concerning ingredients found in dietary supplements. Used for scoring penalties and consumer warnings.
_metadata.scoring_rule: Apply penalty points based on severity_level. Critical: -5, High: -3, Moderate: -1.5, Low: -0.5. Multiple additives are cumulative.

severity_level_definitions.critical: Immediate health hazard, should be avoided entirely (e.g., formaldehyde, colloidal silver)
severity_level_definitions.high: Significant health concerns with strong evidence (e.g., trans fats, carcinogenic dyes, heavy metals)
severity_level_definitions.moderate: Potential health concerns, limit exposure (e.g., artificial sweeteners, high glycemic fillers)
severity_level_definitions.low: Minor concerns, generally safe at typical doses (e.g., some flow agents, natural thickeners)

fields.aliases: Alternative names, E-numbers, chemical names for matching
fields.mechanism_of_harm: Scientific explanation of how the additive causes harm
fields.regulatory_status: Approval status in US, EU, and other jurisdictions
fields.notes: Additional context and recent research findings
fields.references_structured: Structured citations with authority and grade

_metadata.pipeline_integration: Used by enrichment script to apply penalty points for harmful ingredients during scoring. Matches against ingredient names and other_ingredient forms.
sources: PubMed peer-reviewed studies (DOIs provided per entry)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Metabolizes into aspartic acid, phenylalanine, and methanol. Phenylalanine toxic to people with PKU. May affect neurotransmitter balance, trigger headaches, and influence mood. Possible carcinogenic effects through unclear mechanisms.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved with ADI of 50 mg/kg body weight/day
regulatory_status.EU: E951 approved with ADI of 40 mg/kg body weight/day

population_warnings: People with phenylketonuria (PKU) - MUST AVOID - Toxic accumulation of phenylalanine
Pregnant women - Caution advised
People prone to headaches - May trigger migraines
Children - Limit exposure recommended
harmful_additives.notes: An intense artificial sweetener. Classified as possibly carcinogenic to humans (Group 2B) by WHO/IARC in July 2023, confirmed in 2024 monograph. Not suitable for individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU). Linked to headaches, mood changes, and metabolic issues. Despite IARC classification, regulatory agencies maintain current acceptable daily intake levels.

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.2903/j.efsa.2013.3496 - EFSA safety assessment
IARC Monographs Volume 134 (2024) - Aspartame carcinogenicity

change_log.change: No authoritative dose guidance identified; dose_thresholds not added
Normalized aliases/label_tokens and reduced overlap collisions for deterministic supplement matching

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: High in saturated fats (approximately 50%) which may raise LDL cholesterol when consumed in excess. Processed forms may contain contaminants including 3-MCPD esters and glycidyl esters (potential carcinogens formed during refining at high temperatures).

regulatory_status.EU: Approved but with concerns about 3-MCPD and glycidyl ester contaminants
regulatory_status.EFSA: 2018 opinion on contaminants in refined palm oil

population_warnings: People with high cholesterol - May raise LDL levels
Infants and children - Higher exposure to 3-MCPD contaminants per body weight
People with cardiovascular disease - Limit saturated fat intake
harmful_additives.notes: Associated with deforestation and habitat destruction. High in saturated fats (may raise LDL cholesterol if consumed excessively). Some derivatives (e.g., vitamin A palmitate) are benign in small amounts. Processed forms may contain contaminants (3-MCPD, glycidol). Consider sustainability certifications (RSPO) for reduced environmental impact.

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.2903/j.efsa.2018.5083 - EFSA 3-MCPD assessment
DOI: 10.3390/nu12051347 - Palm oil health effects

change_log.change: Normalized aliases/label_tokens and reduced overlap collisions for deterministic supplement matching

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Can be contaminated with asbestos (a known carcinogen) if not properly purified, as talc and asbestos deposits often occur together geologically. Asbestos-free talc may still cause inflammation when particles are inhaled or reach internal tissues. Linked to ovarian cancer and lung cancer.

regulatory_status.US: FDA does not approve but monitors for asbestos contamination; recent recalls due to asbestos
regulatory_status.EU: E553b approved with strict purity requirements
regulatory_status.WHO/IARC: Talc containing asbestos - Group 1 carcinogen; Talc not containing asbestos - Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic)

population_warnings: Women - Ovarian cancer risk with perineal use
Everyone - Lung cancer risk if inhaled
People with respiratory conditions - Avoid inhalation
Infants - Avoid talc-containing products
harmful_additives.notes: An anti-caking agent that can be contaminated with asbestos, a known carcinogen. Linked to ovarian and lung cancer. FDA has found asbestos contamination in some talc products as recently as 2024. Even asbestos-free talc classified as possibly carcinogenic (Group 2B) by IARC.

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.1093/jnci/djw038 - Ovarian cancer association
IARC Monographs Volume 93 - Talc carcinogenicity
ICSC 0329 - Talc (silica and fibre free)

change_log.change: Confidence downgraded due to insufficient citations
No authoritative dose guidance identified; dose_thresholds not added

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Chlorinated artificial sweetener that disrupts gut microbiome composition and diversity. Inhibits farnesoid X receptor signaling and disrupts bile acid metabolism. May induce glucose intolerance and affect insulin response. Releases potentially harmful chlorinated compounds when heated above 120°C (248°F).

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved with ADI of 5 mg/kg body weight/day
regulatory_status.EU: E955 approved with ADI of 15 mg/kg body weight/day
regulatory_status.Canada: Approved as tabletop sweetener and food additive

population_warnings: People with diabetes - May affect glucose metabolism
People with gut issues - Disrupts microbiome
People who bake - Releases harmful compounds when heated
Children - Limit exposure to artificial sweeteners
harmful_additives.notes: An artificial sweetener made by chlorinating sugar. 2024 studies show it disrupts the gut microbiome, inhibits bile acid metabolism, and may induce glucose intolerance. Releases harmful compounds when heated. May affect insulin response despite being non-caloric.

references_structured.citation: WHO guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children (2015)

change_log.change: Confidence downgraded due to insufficient citations

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Metabolized primarily by the liver, bypassing normal glucose regulation. Excessive intake contributes to insulin resistance, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD), metabolic syndrome, and increased triglycerides. May promote visceral fat accumulation and uric acid production.

regulatory_status.WHO: Recommends limiting free sugars to <10% of energy intake

population_warnings: People with diabetes - Worsens insulin resistance
People with metabolic syndrome - Exacerbates condition
People with fatty liver disease - Directly contributes to NAFLD
Everyone - Limit intake to prevent metabolic issues
harmful_additives.notes: A simple sugar that, when consumed in high amounts, is metabolized primarily by the liver and can contribute to insulin resistance, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, and metabolic syndrome. High fructose corn syrup (HFCS) is particularly problematic due to high fructose content and widespread use.

references_structured.citation: WHO guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children (2015)

change_log.change: Confidence downgraded due to insufficient citations
Normalized aliases/label_tokens and reduced overlap collisions for deterministic supplement matching

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Concentrated liquid sugars that cause rapid blood sugar spikes and insulin surges. Contribute to insulin resistance, weight gain, and metabolic dysfunction. High glycemic index leads to energy crashes and increased hunger. Brown rice syrup may contain arsenic.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status for various syrups

population_warnings: People with diabetes - Causes blood sugar spikes
People with metabolic syndrome - Worsens insulin resistance
Infants and children - Rice syrup may contain arsenic
Everyone - Limit intake
harmful_additives.notes: Concentrated liquid sugars used as sweeteners. They have the same metabolic effects as solid sugars, contributing to blood sugar spikes and related health issues. Brown rice syrup has additional concerns due to potential arsenic contamination.

references_structured.citation: WHO guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children (2015)

change_log.change: Confidence downgraded due to insufficient citations
Normalized aliases/label_tokens and reduced overlap collisions for deterministic supplement matching

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Essentially dried corn syrup - pure glucose with extremely high glycemic index (GI 100+). Causes rapid, severe blood sugar spikes. Metabolically equivalent to pure sugar. Used as cheap filler/sweetener in supplements. Contributes to insulin resistance, weight gain, and metabolic dysfunction.
population_warnings: People with diabetes - Extreme blood sugar impact
People with metabolic syndrome - Worsens condition significantly
People with insulin resistance - Exacerbates the problem
Everyone - Essentially sugar, should be minimized
harmful_additives.notes: HIGH RISK: Corn Syrup Solids are dehydrated corn syrup - essentially pure glucose. Has extremely high glycemic index, equivalent to eating pure sugar. Often used as cheap filler in supplements. Indicates low-quality formulation. Should be avoided especially by diabetics and those with metabolic issues.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.1016/j.numecd.2019.05.062 - High GI carbohydrates and metabolic disease

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.1093/ajcn/86.4.895 - Glycemic effects of glucose
DOI: 10.1016/j.numecd.2019.05.062 - High GI carbohydrates and metabolic disease

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Maltodextrin derived from cassava/tapioca. Same metabolic concerns as corn maltodextrin - high glycemic index (GI 85-105), causes rapid blood sugar spikes. May disrupt gut microbiome. Often marketed as 'natural' alternative but metabolically equivalent to other maltodextrins.
population_warnings: People with diabetes - Causes blood sugar spikes
People with metabolic syndrome - Worsens insulin resistance
People with gut issues - May disrupt microbiome
harmful_additives.notes: MODERATE RISK: Maltodextrin derived from cassava. Despite being marketed as 'natural' or 'non-GMO' alternative, has the same high glycemic index and metabolic concerns as corn-based maltodextrin. Used as cheap filler in supplements.

references_structured.citation: WHO guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children (2015)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: When listed simply as 'Tapioca' (not tapioca fiber), indicates refined starch used as filler. High glycemic index, causes blood sugar spikes. Provides empty calories with no nutritional benefit. Sign of low-quality supplement formulation using cheap fillers.
population_warnings: People with diabetes - High glycemic impact
People with metabolic issues - Blood sugar effects
Everyone - Empty calorie filler
harmful_additives.notes: MODERATE RISK: When 'Tapioca' appears as ingredient (not 'tapioca fiber'), it's typically refined starch used as cheap filler. Has high glycemic index and provides no nutritional benefit. Indicates low-quality formulation. Tapioca FIBER is different and acceptable.

references_structured.citation: WHO guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children (2015)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Triggers gut inflammation and disrupts intestinal barrier function. Alters gut microbiota composition. 2024 clinical study shows it induces insulin resistance and inflammatory response in healthy humans. Can cause chronic low-grade intestinal inflammation. May promote inflammatory bowel disease.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status but under review due to safety concerns
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA acceptable but notes concerns about degraded forms

population_warnings: People with IBD or Crohn's disease - May trigger flares and amplify inflammation
People with IBS - May worsen symptoms
People with diabetes or prediabetes - May induce insulin resistance
Everyone - 2024 research shows inflammatory effects in healthy individuals
harmful_additives.notes: A seaweed extract used for thickening and emulsifying. UPGRADED TO HIGH RISK based on 2024 clinical research. BMC Medicine study (November 2024) shows moderate carrageenan intake triggers gut inflammation and insulin resistance in healthy young men. December 2024 study shows it amplifies inflammatory profile in IBD patients. Linked to digestive inflammation, bloating, and symptoms resembling IBS.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.1186/s12916-024-03771-8 - BMC Medicine 2024 clinical study on insulin resistance

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.1186/s12916-024-03771-8 - BMC Medicine 2024 clinical study on insulin resistance

DOI: 10.3389/fnut.2024.1387646 - Gut microbiota disruption 2024
EFSA Journal 2018;16(4):5238 - Re-evaluation of carrageenan (E 407) and processed Eucheuma seaweed (E 407a)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Class III (E150c) and Class IV (E150d) caramel colors are produced with ammonia and may contain 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), a potential carcinogen. 4-MEI has been shown to cause lung, liver, and thyroid tumors in animal studies. May also contain other contaminants from manufacturing process.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved; California Prop 65 limits 4-MEI to 29 mcg/day
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI for 4-MEI is 0.2 mg/kg body weight

population_warnings: Everyone - Potential carcinogen exposure from 4-MEI
Children - Higher exposure per body weight
People consuming cola beverages - High 4-MEI exposure
harmful_additives.notes: Some types (Class III and IV) are produced with ammonia and may contain 4-methylimidazole (4-MEI), a potential carcinogen. California Prop 65 requires warning labels for products with >29 mcg 4-MEI per day. Class I and II are safer alternatives.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Caramel color (21 CFR 73.100)

change_log.change: Confidence downgraded due to insufficient citations
Normalized aliases/label_tokens and reduced overlap collisions for deterministic supplement matching

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Severely disrupts gut microbiome composition and diversity. Promotes chronic low-grade intestinal inflammation and compromises intestinal barrier function. May be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (a carcinogen). 2024-2025 studies show it accelerates age-related cognitive decline via gut-brain axis disruption and drives mucus-penetrating bacteria that cause inflammation.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved for use in foods and supplements
regulatory_status.WHO: ADI not specified but under review

population_warnings: People with IBD or Crohn's disease - May trigger or worsen inflammation
People with gut issues - Severely disrupts microbiome
Older adults - May accelerate cognitive decline
Everyone - Chronic inflammation concerns
harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic emulsifier that may be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (a carcinogen). UPGRADED TO HIGH RISK based on 2024-2025 research showing severe gut microbiome disruption, chronic intestinal inflammation, and links to inflammatory bowel disease. 2024 study shows it accelerates age-related cognitive decline. 2025 research confirms it drives mucus-penetrating microbiota causing chronic inflammation.

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.1016/j.bbi.2024.05.033 - Cognitive decline 2024
DOI: 10.1038/s41467-025-45123-4 - Mucus-penetrating bacteria 2025
DOI: 10.1038/s42003-024-06224-3 - Gut microbiome disruption 2024
21 CFR 172.840 - Polysorbates

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic solvent and carrier that acts as penetration enhancer, potentially increasing absorption of other substances. Can cause allergic reactions, contact dermatitis, and irritation. High doses may cause lactic acidosis, central nervous system depression, and kidney damage. May accumulate with repeated exposure.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status for food use

population_warnings: People with allergies - May cause allergic reactions
People with kidney disease - May accumulate and cause toxicity
Infants and children - Lower tolerance, risk of toxicity
People taking multiple supplements - Cumulative exposure risk
harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic liquid used as a solvent and carrier, often for 'natural flavors'. It is a penetration enhancer and can cause allergic reactions. High doses can be toxic, particularly in vulnerable populations. Widely used but safety concerns exist for chronic exposure.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Propylene glycol (21 CFR 172.1666)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Acts as excitatory neurotransmitter that can overstimulate nerve cells. May cause 'MSG symptom complex' including headaches, flushing, sweating, facial pressure, numbness, and heart palpitations in sensitive individuals. Potential excitotoxic effects on neurons at high doses.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status but requires labeling
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA - No ADI specified but recommends moderation

population_warnings: People sensitive to MSG - May cause symptom complex
People with migraines - May trigger headaches
People with asthma - May worsen symptoms in some cases
Children - Limit exposure to excitotoxins
harmful_additives.notes: A flavor enhancer that acts as an excitotoxin in the brain. Can cause headaches, flushing, sweating, and neurological symptoms in sensitive individuals. Often hidden in ingredients like 'yeast extract' or 'hydrolyzed protein'. 2024 research continues to investigate neurological effects.

references_structured.citation: DOI: 10.1007/s00726-018-2594-9 - MSG safety review
DOI: 10.1016/j.lfs.2020.117659 - Neurotoxicity mechanisms

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Incompletely absorbed in small intestine, leading to osmotic diarrhea and fermentation by gut bacteria. Causes bloating, gas, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. May disrupt gut microbiome. Different sugar alcohols have varying degrees of absorption and tolerance.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status; requires warning label if >50g/day may cause laxative effect
regulatory_status.WHO: Various ADIs depending on specific polyol

population_warnings: People with IBS - May trigger severe symptoms
People with digestive issues - Causes bloating and diarrhea
Children - Lower tolerance, higher risk of diarrhea
Everyone - Limit intake to avoid digestive distress
harmful_additives.notes: Sweeteners that are not fully absorbed by the body. Can cause significant digestive issues like bloating, gas, and diarrhea, especially when consumed in moderate to large amounts. Tolerance varies by individual and type of sugar alcohol. Xylitol is toxic to dogs. UPGRADED TO MODERATE RISK due to significant digestive effects.

references_structured.citation: WHO guideline: Sugars intake for adults and children (2015)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic polymer (modified cellulose) that swells rapidly in water. Generally considered safe but may cause bloating, gas, or digestive discomfort in sensitive individuals. Not absorbed systemically.
population_warnings: People with digestive sensitivity - May cause bloating
People with cellulose allergies - Rare but possible reactions
harmful_additives.notes: A 'superdisintegrant' that swells rapidly in water to help tablets and capsules break apart in the digestive system. It is a synthetic polymer (modified cellulose) that may cause bloating or other digestive issues in sensitive individuals. Generally well-tolerated.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Croscarmellose sodium (21 CFR 172.330)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic polymer largely unabsorbed by the body. As a synthetic microplastic, theoretical concerns exist about potential accumulation in tissues with long-term exposure, though evidence is limited. May cause minor digestive effects in sensitive individuals.
population_warnings: People concerned about microplastics - Theoretical accumulation risk
People with digestive sensitivity - May cause minor effects
harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic polymer and 'superdisintegrant' used to promote rapid tablet dissolution. It is largely unabsorbed, but as a synthetic microplastic, some concerns exist about potential accumulation in tissues with long-term exposure. Generally considered safe.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Crospovidone (21 CFR 172.375)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Starches chemically or physically altered to improve function. Processing may introduce trace contaminants or residual chemicals. Some individuals may have sensitivities to specific modification processes. Generally considered safe at typical exposure levels.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status for various modified starches
regulatory_status.WHO: Various ADIs depending on modification type

population_warnings: People with gluten sensitivity - Wheat-based modified starch may contain gluten
People with corn allergies - Corn-based modified starch may cause reactions
People concerned about GMOs - Often derived from GMO crops
harmful_additives.notes: Starches that have been chemically or physically altered to improve function. The processing can introduce unknown contaminants and may involve chemicals. Generally considered safe but source matters for people with allergies.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Modified starch (21 CFR 172.892)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Large-scale 2023-2024 studies link elevated erythritol levels to increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events including heart attack and stroke. May enhance platelet reactivity and thrombosis risk. Can cause digestive distress including bloating, gas, and diarrhea at moderate to high doses.
population_warnings: People with cardiovascular disease - Increased heart attack and stroke risk
People at risk for blood clots - May enhance platelet reactivity
People with IBS - May cause digestive distress
Everyone - Cardiovascular concerns from recent research
harmful_additives.notes: A sugar alcohol used as a zero-calorie sweetener. UPGRADED TO MODERATE RISK based on 2023-2024 large-scale studies linking it to increased risk of heart attack and stroke. Research shows it may enhance platelet reactivity and thrombosis. Can also cause digestive distress. Despite GRAS status, growing safety concerns.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: While copper is an essential mineral, cupric sulfate is a synthetic inorganic form. Can cause gastrointestinal irritation, nausea, and vomiting at high doses. Excessive copper intake may lead to liver damage over time. However, toxicity primarily occurs at doses far exceeding typical supplement amounts.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved as copper source in supplements and foods
regulatory_status.WHO: Copper is essential nutrient; UL is 10 mg/day for adults

population_warnings: People with Wilson's disease - MUST AVOID - Cannot metabolize copper properly
People with liver disease - May accumulate copper
People taking high-dose copper supplements - Risk of toxicity
harmful_additives.notes: While copper is an essential mineral, cupric sulfate is a synthetic form. DOWNGRADED TO LOW RISK as copper is essential and toxicity only occurs at high doses far exceeding normal supplement use. Generally safe at recommended levels. Main concern is for people with Wilson's disease who cannot metabolize copper.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Cupric sulfate (21 CFR 172.310)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Acts as endocrine disruptors by mimicking estrogen, potentially interfering with hormone signaling pathways. Can leach from plastic packaging into supplements. May have similar or worse effects than BPA.
population_warnings: Pregnant women - May affect fetal development
Children - Potential developmental effects
People with hormone-sensitive conditions
harmful_additives.notes: Common BPA substitutes found in 'BPA-free' plastic packaging. Research suggests they may be as harmful as or worse than BPA, with similar endocrine-disrupting properties.
scientific_references: DOI: 10.1289/EHP2716 - Comparative analysis of BPA alternatives

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic forms may have different bioavailability and metabolism. Synthetic folic acid requires conversion to active form, which can be inefficient in some people. Synthetic vitamin E (dl-alpha) has lower biological activity and may interfere with natural form absorption.
population_warnings: People with MTHFR mutations - May have difficulty processing synthetic folic acid
Pregnant women - Should prefer natural folate forms
People with cardiovascular conditions - Caution with synthetic vitamin E
harmful_additives.notes: Synthetic vitamins often have lower bioavailability and may not provide the same benefits as natural forms. Some may accumulate in tissues or interfere with natural vitamin metabolism.

references_structured.citation: FDA Food Additive Status List - Synthetic vitamins (21 CFR 172.360)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Accumulates in bones and soft tissues. Crosses blood-brain barrier. Interferes with enzyme function and cellular processes. Particularly dangerous to neural development. Can replace calcium in bones and disrupt multiple body systems.

regulatory_status.US: FDA action level of 5 mcg/day from supplements

Pregnant women - Can cross placental barrier
Everyone - Cumulative toxin with no safe level
harmful_additives.notes: One of the most dangerous heavy metal contaminants. No safe level of exposure. Common in supplements from contaminated soil or poor manufacturing practices. Causes irreversible neurological damage.

People with anemia - May worsen condition

population_warnings: People avoiding artificial dyes - Source is unclear
harmful_additives.notes: Low-severity flag for unspecified color listings. Penalize lightly unless explicit dye tokens indicate artificial colorants.

references_structured.citation: EFSA Journal 2015;13(2):4037 - Re-evaluation of carmine (E 120)

change_log.change: Confidence downgraded due to insufficient citations
Normalized aliases/label_tokens and reduced overlap collisions for deterministic supplement matching
